summary:
The Maccabean Revolt (167–160 BC) was a Jewish uprising against the [[event:1155|Antiochus IV Epiphanes]]' Seleucid policy of forced Hellenization, led by the Maccabee family (the Hasmoneans), which succeeded in rededicated the defiled Temple in Jerusalem — an event commemorated annually in the Jewish holiday of Hanukkah — and ultimately led to the independent [[event:1147|Hasmonean Kingdom]].

Antiochus IV's decree prohibiting Jewish religious practice and his installation of a statue of Zeus in the [[event:1150|Second Temple of Jerusalem]] provoked violent resistance. The elderly priest Mattathias began the revolt by killing a Jewish man who complied with Seleucid demands for sacrifice. His son Judas Maccabeus (the name means 'the Hammer') emerged as a brilliant guerrilla commander, winning a series of battles against Seleucid forces despite massive disadvantages in numbers and equipment. In 164 BC the Maccabees recaptured Jerusalem and rededicated the Temple — Hanukkah commemorates the miraculous oil said to have burned for eight days during this rededication. The revolt is historically significant as one of the first successful resistance movements against cultural assimilation, and the Books of Maccabees provide important evidence for understanding Hellenistic Judaism and Second Temple period history.
